macro_segments.title: Wife's Obligation to Nurse: The Debate Between Beit Shammai and Beit Hillel

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the nursing obligation (umeneket beno) and the vow that prevents nursing
Types of vows preventing nursing and the dispute about who benefits from the vow
Beit Shammai vs. Beit Hillel: Does the mother have an obligation to nurse?
Post-divorce nursing: When a vow is binding and when obligation continues
Reassessment: Is the dispute about obligation or about blame and financial responsibility?
Sakana (danger) to the child overrides vows; distinction between vow-binding and responsibility
The case where vow is affirmed by husband: Beit Shammai vs Beit Hillel on fault

macro_segments.title: Recognition of Mother and Continuation of Nursing After Divorce

micro_segments.title: When does a child recognize the mother and refuse other nursemaids?
Dispute among Amoraim: 3 months (Rav), 30 days (Shmuel), 50 days (Rav Yitzchak)
Rav Dimi's correction: Case-by-case determination rather than fixed ages
Story of Shmuel's lineup test to determine if child recognizes mother
Response to blind child: Other senses (smell, taste) enable recognition

macro_segments.title: Duration and Appropriateness of Nursing: Biblical and Rabbinic Limits

micro_segments.title: Baraita on nursing duration: 24 months normal; Rabbi Eliezer says beyond is yonek sheket
Rabbi Yehoshua permits up to 4-5 years; debate if truly unlimited or capped
Distinction between nursing act and expressed milk: Product vs. act
Proof from kashrut of human blood and milk: Neither is non-kosher
Reverse principle by blood: Cannot drink expressed blood, but can suck finger
Halakha follows Rabbi Yehoshua: allowed up to ~4-5 years

macro_segments.title: Suckling Directly from Animal on Shabbat and Other Shabbat Cases

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Merinus: Sick person may suckle directly from cow udder on Shabbat
Reason: Suckling is shinui from normal milking; permitted in place of tzaar
Application to mothers expressing milk: Whether it counts as shinui or natural
Nachum Ish Galia: Pushing drain pipe with foot on Shabbat in case of roof damage

macro_segments.title: Obligation to Continue Nursing After Husband's Death Within 24 Months

micro_segments.title: If husband dies within 24 months: Mother forbidden to accept kiddushin or nissuin
Reason: Pregnancy would compromise milk supply for nursing child
Rabbi Meir: 24 months obligation; Rabbi Yehuda: 18 months (interpreted as 15)
Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel reconciliation: Counts 3 months pregnancy time
Ulla rules like Rabbi Yehuda; Mar Okva cites Rabbi Chanina allowing 15 months
Abaye's sharecropper case: Rav Yosef rules 24 months strictly; Abaye runs after him
Teaching: Don't rule confidently without consulting your rebbe

macro_segments.title: Exceptions to 24-Month Wait: Weaning, Nursemaid, or Child's Death

micro_segments.title: Baraita: If child given to nursemaid, weaned, or died, mother may remarry immediately
Rav Pappa & Rav Huna attempt to rule like baraita; elderly woman contradicts them
Rav Nachman allowed women of Resh Galuta but others must wait; fear of reversing decision
Analogy to three-month wait for paternity: Should apply consistently
Baraita on women who must wait 3 months: Infertile, elderly, minor, or miscarried
Rabbi Meir: Always 3 months; Rabbi Yosi: Immediate if no concern; Halakha: Rabbi Meir
No contradiction: Always rule 24 months by child nursing, except if child dies
Mar bar Rav Ashi: Even if child dies, forbid remarriage (fear of infanticide)
Gemara rejects: Woman was crazy; normal women do not kill children
